index.html.title: index
index.html.description:
The Agrippa Project — Issue I ~ by The Agrippa Project
In 1531, Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa of Nettesheim published a book so dangerous it required the reader's wisdom as a precondition of understanding. The Three Books of Occult Philosophy, as he titled it, would go on to be burned in Paris, reprinted endlessly, plagiarized by grimoire authors, inspire a playwright to name his devil after its author, and eventually become the ur-text of Western occultism. It also, by some accident of intellectual history, became the occasion for one of the most productive scholarly disputes in modern Renaissance studies. lehrich-language-demons-angels-2003
The book is, depending on whom you ask, a compendium; a coherent philosophical argument; a semiotic machine; a piece of Christian apologetics; a skeptical exercise; or a trap. It contains natural-magic, ceremonial magic, alchemy, Kabbalah, astrology, and enough theological heterodoxy to get its author excommunicated twice over. It has been read by Catholics and Protestants, Enlightenment skeptics and Romantic poets, crowley-aleister and Goethe. Each reader has emerged convinced that Agrippa was, essentially, on their side. He was not. He was on all sides simultaneously — or as Lehrich has recently argued, the book's secret was not a hidden doctrine but the reading practice itself.This is the paradox at the heart of the Agrippa industry: a text that generates incompatible philosophies from incompatible philosophers, all of whom claim interpretive priority. The question this issue tries to hold without resolving is whether this is a failure of the text or its greatest achievement. Continue Read..

perrone-compagni-vittoria — Scholar; editor of De occulta philosophia; argues for coherent Agrippa
pomponazzi-pietro — Aristotelian naturalist; De incantationibus; natural magic vs. demonic magic distinction
postel-guillaume — Only figure to carry Agrippa's magia reformata forward; in a Kabbalistic direction
proclus — Greek Neoplatonist; actual source for Renaissance magical philosophy; Chaldaean Oracles, not Hermetica
pseudo-geber — Anonymous alchemist; author of Summa perfectionis; Agrippa's alchemical source
reuchlin-johannes — Pioneer of Christian Kabbalah; De Verbo Mirifico and De Arte Cabbalistica; key influence on Agrippa's magical reform
sidney-philip — Elizabethan poet; Dee's pupil; central figure of the Sidney circle; connection to Spenser's Faerie Queene
sledge-justin — YouTube historian; 14-lecture course on Agrippa; emphasizes reading Agrippa as a philosopher
walker-daniel-pickering — Warburg scholar; correct alternative to Yates; identified pneuma/spiritus as magic's theoretical foundation
weyer-johann — Johann Weyer; Agrippa's pupil; wrote De Praestigiis Daemonum against witchcraft
yates-frances — Historian; author of Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition (1964) and The Occult Philosophy in the Elizabethan Age (1979); Yates thesis
zoroaster — Persian sage; founding figure of prisca theologia; genealogical anchor of magic's respectability
alchemy-agrippa — Alchemical chapters in De occulta philosophia (1533); integral, not abandoned
analog-signification — Lehrich's theory; signs have scalar (not binary) power to refer; sigils achieve maximum referential lock
christian-cabala — Christian use of Jewish Kabbalah for religious reform; Pico, Reuchlin, Agrippa, Dee
demonic-magic — Magic working through demons; Pomponazzi's naturalistic rejection; the boundary between natural and demonic
dispersa-intentio — Agrippa's deliberate concealment technique; scattered across the text
divine-generation — Lazzarelli's doctrine; gnosis grants power to create souls
elizabethan-age — Yates's argument; Hermetic-Cabalist culture underpinning Spenser, Dee, Sidney, Marlowe
faith-reason-harmony — Agrippa's central principle; reason grounded in faith
gnosis — Superior knowledge of self and God; central to the Hermetica
hermetic-deification — Apotheosis through spiritual regeneration; the God-man
hermetism — Renaissance Hermetic tradition; scope and contested historiography; Copenhaver's critique of "Hermetic" as misnomer
magic-learned — Renaissance learned magic tradition; Yates thesis, Hanegraaff critique, and Copenhaver's "spiritual magic"
magical-objects — Stones, plants, animals as magical tools; hypertextualization of magical objects in book culture
microcosm-man — Man as image of world and God; contains all levels of reality
natural-magic — Magic grounded in natural philosophy; occult qualities; Copenhaver's emphasis
paganism-as-category — Hanegraaff's key insight; "paganism" is the unnamed core driving Western esotericism's history
pneuma-spiritus — The cosmic medium linking soul to cosmos; the key mechanism of Ficinian spiritual magic
prisca-theologia — Ancient divine wisdom tradition; genealogy of prophet-philosophers
rejected-knowledge — Hanegraaff's polemical category; knowledge expelled from the academy; the Other defining academic identity
restauratio-magicae — Agrippa's program; restoration of magic through cultural reform
ritual-magic — Ceremonial magic invoking supernatural beings; Campanella's politicized ritual; Agrippa's Book III; Lehrich's writing-centered ritual
sigils-magical-characters — Inscribed magical signs; core mechanism of Agrippa's magic; not decorative but constitutive
skepticism-agrippa — Agrippa's radical skeptical turn in De Incertitudine; influence on Descartes
spiritual-magic — Walker's term; Ficinian magic operating through pneuma; Copenhaver's corrective to Yates
spiritual-regeneration — Rebirth from God; reversal of the Fall through gnosis
threefold-world — Agrippa's cosmology; elemental, celestial, and intellectual worlds interpenetrating
western-esotericism — Hanegraaff's defining concept; rejected knowledge expelled from the academy; the historiographical field defined by exclusion
writing-centered-ritual — Lehrich's theory; ritual as inscription rather than speech; inverts standard ritual theory
yates-thesis — Frances Yates's argument; critique by Hanegraaff and Copenhaver; Walker over Yates
agrippa-de-occulta-philosophia-1533-purdue — Primary source; Agrippa's own text; threefold world, threefold magic, theurgic ascent, explicit dispersed intentions
compagni-dispersa-intentio-2000 — Alchemy; reconciliation of De vanitate and De occulta philosophia
copenhaver-magic-western-culture-2015 — Magic as classical philosophical tradition; Copenhaver's critique of Yates; Walker over Yates; Pomponazzi, Campanella, pneuma theory
hanegraaff-better-than-magic-2009 — Yates thesis critique; Lazzarellian gnosis; divine generation
hanegraaff-esotericism-academy-2012 — Synoptic history of Western esotericism as rejected knowledge; prisca theologia and the paganism factor; academic recovery from Renaissance to Faivre
lehrich-language-demons-angels-2003 — Sustained close-reading of DOP; philosophical coherence of Agrippa; writing-centered ritual; sigils and analog signification
sledge-lecture-14-transcript — Sledge's 14-lecture course on Agrippa; philosophy vs. legend; magia reformata failure; missing end of Three Books
yates-giordano-bruno-hermetic-tradition-1964 — The Yates thesis; Hermetism as central Renaissance tradition; Pico's Conclusiones Magicae; Agrippa's Book VII survey
yates-occult-philosophy-elizabethan-age-1979 — Elizabethan age as Hermetic-Cabalist culture; Dee, Spenser, Marlowe, Sidney circle

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar
